title: 'Plane' and the Top 10 Sky Trash Movies
format: Gerard Butler crash-lands in the Philippines in Plane, which sends Sean and Chris into a full audit of the airplane-actioner genre, capped by dueling top-5 sky trash countdowns.

segments.heading: The DC Movie Universe, Rebooted
segments.note: cold open, on James Gunn and Peter Safran's new slate

films.title: Justice League
films.note: The jumping-off joke for the segment: one host claims to have worked on 'the Snyder Cut' and is now 'restoring' the Whedon Cut, i.e. the original theatrical version.

films.title: The Suicide Squad
films.note: Referenced in passing: Gunn turned down a podcast interview request during this film's press cycle.

films.title: Guardians of the Galaxy Vol. 3
films.note: Sean admits to being the one Big Picture listener still excited for it.

films.title: Shazam! Fury of the Gods
films.note: Out that same week; noted for Zachary Levi's promotional push.

films.title: The Flash
films.note: Gunn had already called it one of the greatest superhero movies ever made, despite not having made it himself.

films.title: Blue Beetle
films.note: Gunn's own review, per the hosts: 'that exists over there, good luck to them.'

films.title: Aquaman and the Lost Kingdom
films.note: Christmas 2023 release; Sean says he liked the first one and is looking forward to it.

films.title: The Fugitive
films.note: The Tommy Lee Jones reference for how little patience is left for one more extended-universe rollout: 'I don't care.'

films.title: Plane
films.note: Gerard Butler's Captain Brody Torrance flies a nearly empty New Year's Eve flight from Singapore to Tokyo through a lightning storm and crash-lands on Jolo Island in the Philippines, a real and genuinely lawless place the film doesn't bother renaming. One of the fourteen passengers is a shackled murderer (Mike Colter) being extradited; once the island's militia shows up, the movie turns into a Raid-style hand-to-hand action picture for its back half. Both hosts wanted more time in the plane and more comic relief from the supporting cast (Tony Goldwyn's fixer, Paul Ben-Victor's cheapo-airline owner); Chris liked it more than Sean, who found it a little too straight-faced for how thin the premise is.

films.title: Greenland
films.note: The Gerard Butler/Ric Roman Waugh disaster movie the hosts think Plane is chasing: also dead serious, and released into a moment primed to take it that way.

films.title: Kandahar
films.note: Butler and Waugh's next collaboration, an undercover-CIA-operative thriller also featuring Olivia-Mai Barrett as his daughter (rendered as 'Olivia Mae Barrett' in the transcript).

segments.heading: What Actually Counts as SkyTrash?
segments.note: drawing the genre's boundaries before the countdown

films.title: Top Gun
films.note: The hosts' clean definition of the genre: thin story, high action, minimal characterization, maximum jargon.

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: The un-trashy counterexample: all the same ingredients, but weighted down with real feeling about the characters and the decades of movies behind them.

films.title: Iron Eagle
films.note: Chris's honorable mention: teenagers from Bakersfield launch an air raid on Libya, the same year and same basic plot as Top Gun, but dumber.

films.title: Wings
films.note: Cited as an early ancestor of the genre, not far off from the sky trash formula decades later.

films.title: The Bridges at Toko-Ri
films.note: Named among the postwar aerial pictures that built the genre's template.

films.title: The Aviator
films.note: Brought up for Howard Hughes's own fascination with flight as cinematic spectacle.

films.title: Black Sunday
films.note: The hinge point the hosts identify: mid-'70s hijacking anxiety starts bleeding into the genre, through the '80s and beyond.

films.title: Sully
films.note: A boundary case for both hosts. Chris doesn't count it as sky trash; Sean does, arguing the film invents a kangaroo-court NTSB hearing to manufacture stakes that never existed.

films.title: Flight
films.note: The other boundary case; both agree this one qualifies, on the strength of a plane crash sequence they call one of the best ever shot, followed by two hours of Denzel Washington in a bender.

films.title: Fearless
films.note: Peter Weir's plane-crash-aftermath drama, explicitly ruled out: 'not sky trash,' though a clear cousin of Flight.

films.title: The Dark Knight Rises
films.note: Its cold-open plane hijacking is singled out as genuinely great; the rest of the movie is mocked as incoherent.

films.title: Asterix & Obelix: The Middle Kingdom
films.note: A tangent about the French film industry pinning its hopes on this Guillaume Canet/Vincent Cassel/Marion Cotillard blockbuster.

films.title: World War Z
films.note: Has a great plane sequence, per Chris, but doesn't clear the sky trash bar on its own.

films.title: F9
films.note: The one where the Fast franchise finally went to space.

films.title: Now You See Me 2
films.note: A rewatchables request Sean says he gets constantly, brought up while tracing director Jon M. Chu's follow-up path.

films.title: Bridesmaids
films.note: Praised for one of the great comedic airplane sequences, via Melissa McCarthy's character.

films.title: Midnight Run
films.note: The steak-and-lobster surf-and-turf scene on the plane gets a shoutout.

films.title: Twilight Zone: The Movie
films.note: The George Miller/John Lithgow segment with the gremlin on the wing, distinguished from the John Landis segment's real-life tragedy.

films.title: Snakes on a Plane
films.note: The viral title that became a movie; both agree the title did more work than the film itself.

films.title: Passenger 57
films.note: Ruled 'fun, but not good,' and more of a Wesley Snipes vehicle than a plane movie.

films.title: Air Force One
films.note: No. 1 - a Harrison Ford, Gary Oldman, and Glenn Close film from Wolfgang Petersen that Sean insists is trash the moment the Secret Service traitor gets sucked out the door as the plane goes down.

films.title: Flightplan
films.note: No. 2 - Jodie Foster's Airbus designer wakes up mid-flight to find her daughter missing, with no record she was ever aboard; a Hitchcockian thriller that made $235 million.

films.title: Non-Stop
films.note: No. 3 - Sean's pick for the best modern version of the genre: Liam Neeson, an absurdly stacked cast, and a plot that delivers exactly what the trailer promises, no more and no less.

films.title: Stealth
films.note: No. 4 - Josh Lucas, Jessica Biel, and Jamie Foxx as stealth-bomber pilots phased out by a rogue AI plane; Jamie Foxx's slow-motion death by mountain is the highlight.

films.title: Horizon Line
films.note: No. 5 - Allison Williams learns to fly a small plane after her instructor (Keith David) dies mid-flight of a heart attack, inspired by Sean's own account of his wife studying a pilot's controls out of anxiety.

films.title: Firefox
films.note: No. 1 - Clint Eastwood steals a Soviet fighter jet that fires its weapons only when the pilot thinks in Russian. Released the month Chris was born; he calls it 'kind of bad but also perfect.'

films.title: Red Eye
films.note: No. 2 - Wes Craven's Rachel McAdams/Cillian Murphy thriller, a charming-stranger-turns-serious-criminal plot Chris pairs with Flightplan; came out the same year.

films.title: Flight of the Phoenix
films.note: No. 3 - Dennis Quaid leads a cast including Tyrese, Giovanni Ribisi, and Miranda Otto rebuilding a crashed transport plane out of its own wreckage in the Gobi Desert; a remake of the 1965 Jimmy Stewart film, scripted in part by Scott Frank.

films.title: Executive Decision
films.note: No. 4 - Kurt Russell, Halle Berry, John Leguizamo, and Steven Seagal in a hijacking thriller built around a B-2 stealth bomber docking with a commercial jet mid-flight.

films.title: Flight of the Intruder
films.note: No. 5 - John Milius's last theatrical feature, a Vietnam-set Navy bombing mission with Willem Dafoe, chosen as a tribute to Milius's filmmaking rather than the movie's quality.

segments.heading: Die Hard 2, and What's Next
segments.note: closing arguments, plus teases for future episodes

films.title: Die Hard 2
films.note: The big omission from both lists. The hosts argue it's spiritually sky trash despite being set almost entirely in an airport, on the strength of one of the most traumatic plane crashes either has seen.

films.title: Turbulence
films.note: Ray Liotta as a killer aboard a commercial flight; remembered fondly, then rewatched and found wanting.

films.title: Broken Arrow
films.note: Ruled out for taking place mostly in national parks, not the sky.

films.title: Always
films.note: Spielberg's aerial firefighting drama, brought up via director Brad Anderson's early work; ruled 'more of a bummer' than sky trash.

films.title: Showing Up
films.note: The answer to a teased riddle about an upcoming film built around a fear of falling planes, even though it isn't set on one: Kelly Reichardt's next movie.

films.title: Fracture
films.note: Ryan Gosling and Anthony Hopkins, cited as the model for a future 'courtroom trash' episode.

referenced.group: DC's next slate (announced, not yet made)
John Milius's other films, cited in the Flight of the Intruder pick
W.D. Richter's other screenwriting credits, cited in the Stealth pick
films: The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the 8th Dimension

referenced.group: Jaume Collet-Serra's filmography, cited in the Flightplan/Non-Stop discussion

The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across.title: The Adventures of Buckaroo Banzai Across the 8th Dimension
